We have a day about structure. So almost all of everything that will be talked about today, you can broaden it a little if you wish at Q&A opportunities, which depending on how the day flows, there may not be any during the day, but there is definitely one at the end of the day. And whatever we have one, you come to the table, there's free candy, there's microphones, I get to sit, and that's how we do Q&A. Otherwise, we're really dealing with the architecture of group selling presentations. And pretty much it doesn't matter, although we will talk some specifics about this stage to group, webcast to group, DVD sent in the mail to group. There's some nuance differences. We will get to them. But generally speaking, the structure elements don't change that much, if at all, depending on delivery. A group presentation is a group presentation, is a group presentation, and there are things that help it structurally. So we will go through it beginning, middle, and really be talking about architecture. And so we began on your page 11 with the open. So there's a, we'll go through them in detail, there's a kind of a short list of objectives that have to, that should occur in the opening of a presentation where anybody has the opportunity to stop it. So in one-to-one selling, if you're at somebody's kitchen table face-to-face, and you've unpacked the pot and pan samples all over the table, they really don't have an opportunity to stop that sales presentation abruptly and early, unless they are going to be incredibly rude. And so most of the time that we're selling one-to-one, we have a little looseness at the beginning to establish rapport and have small talk. There's a lot of things we can do one-to-one that we cannot get away with in any environment where they have an opportunity to abruptly stop it. So if they're in a group, they can get up and leave, and they can mentally tune out, even if they are sitting there. If they have a clicker in their hand, they can obviously click. If they're online, they can go elsewhere online. So you have to be really sensitive to the idea that you can lose them very early and never really have an opportunity. And I have people bring me like scripts all the time, and the good stuff starts like eight minutes in, and this is, you know, and they will say, yeah, but when they get here, I said they are not going to get there. They have too many options to check out. So the first job of the open is to stop the listener, viewer, audience member from checking out, because their earliest impulse is often to check out. And there's things you can do to make that worse, by the way, and there's things you can do to help stop it. Second, we want to engage the listener, viewer, and make him think, make him start questioning, asking questions. We want to frame the presentation, the discussion. We want to short-circuit instant negatives, because they will tune out instantly if something is put in front of them that they have a visceral negative reaction to, and we want to create positive interest. That's all the stuff we want to do pretty quick in the open of a group presentation. So what must be established in an open? First thing that has to be established, if they don't already know it, is who is talking to them. Now, even if they know it, we ought not take for granted that they know it. So within the timing of the open, we need to bring, zip through as many of these things as possible. So that they are reminded, even if they knew it, that this is who's presenting to them. James just raised the issue, which we have talked about, well, I've talked about it many times before, but we talked about it in prior two days, that really in selling anything to anybody, but the more you climb up the affluence ladder, the more important it is, and so forth. Really, the who you are is more important than what you sell. People make more decisions based on who, than on what. And so these are all the things that you could be, so celebrity. So somehow, as early as possible, if you are famous, you want to remind them that you are famous. Now, we have, again, environmental, situational things we can do, right? So if we are doing it in a room, you have to give some thought about how would a celebrity approach this room. So when I was speaking and selling, even from the platform, even in small, not Peter Lo venues, but the Kansas Association of What's In Soul, and there's 50 people in the room, almost without exception, I did not hang out as people were coming in the door and greet them and talk to them and make nice to them, because that's not what a celebrity does. A celebrity is backstage, it appears on stage when it's time for them to perform. Furthermore, they usually have an opening act. So ideally, somebody else is up there for 15 minutes doing something before it's me. So you have to think about what you can do in whatever media environment you are in that telegraphs you're a celebrity and or avoids undermining the idea that you are a celebrity. A five minute film clip of, you know, so if you go back and you, so you look at infomercials, for example, where our time is precious, we have 28 and a half minutes, that's all we got, you can't stretch it, you can't do anything about it, you got 28 and a half minutes, you got two call to actions in the middle and a short one at the end, so really you're down to about 24 minutes, no matter what, you got to divide it into three segments. The first segment is where you have to do all this establishing stuff, you get to abbreviate it at the start of the second segment and the third segment, so really you're selling time and the first segment is the shortest because you have to do this, so if you go all the way back and you look at thinking of a rich, you can't just, and at the time of thinking of a rich in 87, most buyers 40 years and up would instantly know who Fran Tarkenton is, but you can't take that for granted and if you're paying money for Fran, you can't just stick Fran out there and assume everybody knows who he is, so you got to give up a couple minutes of that open to establishing he's a celebrity, he's a famous athlete, he's all the stuff they need to know about him, if you are or whoever it is you're presenting is an authority, you got to get that across somehow too in the open, if they have enormous credibility in this subject area, you got to get that across, if they have affinity with the audience, you got to get that across, so if Ben Glass is an attorney, he markets only two attorneys and he's driving only attorneys to his webcast, still it's good to remind them that he's an attorney, not a non-attorney, and that he's an extremely successful attorney and that he has a so that has to be done in that opening no matter what was done before they got there. Think of the presentation no matter how good a job you do in advance of it, think of it as happening in a vacuum, as if they saw nothing before they got there, they heard nothing before they got there, they have fallen out of the sky into the room or in front of the screen and now what do they need to know for this presentation to be effective? If you sell by being likable, not everybody does, some people sell in fact by the opposite, but most people are more effective if the people buying from them like them, then you have to figure out a way to establish likability. You know, Nixon's checker speech carried the dog, I mean there's a reason for that. Role voice personality, you have to establish a sense for them of who this is talking to them that is going to be consistent throughout. So some important cautions. The better known you are to your audience, the less of this you must establish, you still ought not assume any of it as pre-existing. You can short it, but you ought not eliminate any of it. And if you sell to the same people over and over again, you will be sorely tempted to assume it. If for no other reason, then you get tired of telling it. But there's an enormous temptation to also believe they got it. They know, I grew up in a log cabin, I was raised by wolves, I was they already know this, and so there's this enormous temptation to think we don't need to say it anymore. And every time you do that, you will find it compromises your effectiveness. Because they need this stuff re-established as a precursor to being sold to. And if you assume it's there, even though we know each other, if you assume it's there, we'd have a lot, if you go back and look at the brochure that brought you here, there'd be a lot less people in the room if we left out all of the reasons why you should listen to me about this. Most of those reasons you already knew. But still, there's certain precursors to selling you something that need to be said. We also must establish why are you talking to them about this now? So you might think of this as relevance and has timely relevance. So there should be a reason why you are presenting this, and there should be a reason why you are presenting it now. Now this argues against evergreen presentations. However, if the person is new to it, and there's an evergreen timing reason, then the presentation can be evergreen. Often they can last for three or four years, but you should be sensitive to market conditions, economic conditions, trauma going on in your marketplace. So in a general sense, we have bad economies, we have mediocre economies, and we have good economies. Most of the time the condition lasts at least for a few years, so evergreen is a fungible definition. But three or four years out of a presentation is pretty good. You're probably not going to get 10, 15, or 20 anymore out of a presentation. And the change in the economy, the change within an industry that you sell to may require you to go tweak it. Relevance is fine. You at least need that. You at least need sound reasons why you are talking to them about X. It's even better if you have a timing relevance reason for talking to them about X. And if for no other reason for that, you may be a long-term thinker. And if you are, great. But most of the people we sell to are very short-term thinkers. They have a pretty small frame of reference. They are into what is affecting them today, this week, this month. They look at the world through a pretty small window. And if you're standing too far to the side of the window, they don't see you at all. So timing relevance is useful. So best if there's a current or impending event or opportunity or trauma that you can hook your reason for being here talking to you about X now too, then that's great. That's maybe the best of the choices. Next would be the new amazing discovery breakthrough, piece of technology. If you're in health, the discovery that ground up seaweed, taken in sufficient concentration. This has just been found out in France by some doctor. So that's sort of on the same par. Sometimes you're there because they are reaching a certain life benchmark. So that can work for you. But it is best if you have reasons why and you establish them early. You have to establish why they should listen to you about this thing. And implied in that is why they should listen to you versus anybody else. So most of us have comparables and competitors. So maybe I don't have a direct competitor, but there are clearly other people you could listen to about almost any subject that I ever talk about. So we have to hunt for the reason why you should listen to me versus all the others as well as why you should listen to me. And again, this has to be established fairly quickly. In our infomercial world, it may be one sentence, but it is said. So if we stick Frank Tarkenton on to thinking of a rich, that only works. If it's said by him or by somebody, the reason I'm so excited about talking to you about thinking of a rich is because I'm a champion. I'm a winner. I've been all about winning my entire life and I know what it takes to win. And here are 17 secrets about what it takes to win. If you don't do that, then you might as well have a completely irrelevant celebrity up there because you haven't hooked them together. So you need that bridge early. You need it very early why they should stay tuned. So a real common mistake is, again, the assumption that they will stay tuned. So let's say it's your own seminar and they're coming to a physical room. So you're Ari, Craig, a lot of you guys are in this position. So somebody has registered to come see you, whether for free or pay, right? And they have gotten in a car. It's even better if they got on an airplane, of course, but they have gotten in a car and they have come to a physical place to see you. It's an easy assumption to make that they are going to come in, sit down, and they are going to stay tuned. Bad assumption because, generally speaking, people are badly behaved. They don't have any discipline. They don't have any power or concentration. They're easily distracted. So I was in an environment about a year ago, two years ago, audience about twice this size. I'm not anybody else's audience, but I'm like the big dog of the day. And people have come to be there in the room ostensibly to see and hear me. And as I am walking up the side to come up on stage, I'm looking at the rows. And everybody's got their little doohiggies open, okay? They are not like at an anticipatory Dan Kennedy site getting ready for the wonderful two hours they are going to spend with Dan. They are every place else you can think of. I mean, I looked at every row. They're watching a movie. They're checking their social media. They're at Zappos buying something. Two porn sites. So even in an environment where you have brought them to you and they have come to you, there is still a bunch of competition for their attention, including right at their fingertips, naked people. Good looking naked people, right? I can't compete with that on any level. So I know they're trying not to stay tuned. In the low events, as many of you know, as the last speaker of the day, and there was a horrible stampede, because people stayed to see the last famous guy, right? So whoever was before me, Colin Powell, General Schwarzkopf, President Bush, whatever, they stayed in their head. We're going to stay for this last famous person. In some cases, only long enough to say we saw him. So they're marching out during the last famous person, more for some than others. But then when that last famous person is finishing, look out, right? And so I wasn't prepared for it the first time. But I started to prepare for it by the second time, because it's $297 going out the door with every one of those people, and there's a shitload of them, and people could get hurt. So we really worked on how can we get them to stop, quickly rethink, do I want to leave, and sit back down and stay tuned for a few minutes. So still like sitting like this, hand on bag, but stop. Now I got a few minute chance. They tuned into a webcast, you got a few minute chance. What could we do? Even if they came, they came specifically, right? To the webcast, you still only have a few minute chance. So the why they should stay tuned, you've got to figure out what that is, and everything that you can possibly do to help it. This is your list. One is, of course, quick promises. So the copywriting equivalent of this is the top one-third of the first page or of the ad. So it's the pre-head, the headline, and the sub-head. That's what it is. It's the three quick promises. I never like to script more than five, because I think people forgot the first one by the time you get to the fifth one. So three quick promises that will stop somebody in their tracks. That will all, that's interesting, that's exciting. I do want to hear that. And they literally are making the decision to, okay, I'll give him a little more time now because of these promises. You have a great personal relevant statement for them. So if you have a tight audience, so if you have driven to a webcast out of a narrow source, right, and you know age, demographic, pain points, you know these things about your audience, then you can craft the narrow, if you are statement, that the person says, yes, I am, too, and then that buys you some stay tuned for a little while longer. Now that's important. So as a side point, the broader the audience you put yourself in front of, the more diverse the audience you put yourself in front of to make any kind of a group presentation, the less you have the ability to use that tactic and the harder your job is. So if Ben can figure out how to drive only lawyers to a webcast who have been in practice for at least 25 years and have other attorneys working for them and have a family and he knows that's the case, then he can say, because they don't need to know he knows it, he can say, if you are an attorney who's been in practice for no less than 20 years and you have attorneys working for you that frustrate the hell out of you and you have a family who wants you at home more than you are, you especially need to stay tuned because he can say that. If he's driven any and every attorney from the world to the same webcast, he doesn't dare say it because he immediately gets the majority of the viewers to tune out. So there's a link here to what audience you collect to whether or not you can use this tactic, but it's a very powerful tactic. Proactive questions, so you can frame your three promises more as questions. If on the top ten list of things that bother you in life, you need to stay tuned. If a magic genie appeared and offered you three wishes and the wishes were, boom, boom, boom, you need to stay tuned so you can frame it as questions. You can use pure curiosity. I don't like it much, but you do see it used where they have been driven to a presentation not knowing what the thing is that they're going to discover and the open still keeps it a mystery, but the curiosity about it is about what it is and about the benefits that it delivers. So if there was a magic pill that you could get and you could take that instantly eliminated body odor, bad breath, made you a charismatic individual, multiplied your sex appeal and caused money to fall out of trees and hit you on the head, would you like the pill? So you can do that kind of a magical, mystical approach, breaking news. If you are doing stuff live, whether through web media, human media, phone media, radio, TV, if you're doing it live, then you have an opportunity to use breaking news, but be careful, it has to be breaking news that your audience knows about. And increasingly, fewer and fewer and fewer people know about fewer and fewer things in the news. So it's only good breaking news if your audience knows about it and is concerned about it. So who won American Idol last night? Generally, a whole lot more people know that and are talking about that the next morning than know that we just invaded Iraq for the fifth time. This, a huge number, will not even be aware of. If you watched Leno at all in the late years of Leno and you paid attention, you saw year to year to year, more and more explaining of who or what he was talking about to make sure that you got the joke. If you go back and watch a Carson monologue, he never did it. Everybody kind of, there was a consensus knowledge in America of who was president and who was vice president. For example, you know, and who we may have invaded that month. I mean, there was sort of everybody kind of knew that stuff. And so Carson didn't have to explain much. If you looked at Leno and you went and picked two shows from four years ago and two shows from three years ago and two shows from two years ago, and you just look for this, you'll see that the amount of explaining of the setup of the joke increased because he was accurately fearful that huge numbers of viewers wouldn't have the reference for the joke. I mean, and if you want to talk about Biden, see, you can't say Biden because, huh, you got it. The current vice president of the United States, Joe Botsy, you got to do that now. So breaking news only works if you're certain it's a news event that is news to them, to the folks that you are selling to. Now in a niche market, it's, you can't even assume, see, Craig can't even assume that real estate agents know the market is getting better. Now, I think that's a current economic fact in some markets more so than others, but you can't assume it. They don't watch Fox Financial. They don't necessarily read their trade journals. They don't, eight of them in an office, tell each other how things are going. So if he's going to use breaking news that housing sales improved by 6.2% last month, the best improvement in a single month in four years, that's very relevant to selling to them, but he has to assume they don't know any of that. And he's going to have to take the time to say that if he's going to use that piece of breaking news to get somebody to stay tuned, same sort of thing with a warning of threat or danger. You can't assume, some of you just saw I wrote, and in your sample, we'll get to it. You have some extra samples to take home, way at the back of your manual. There's a sample in there of a PowerPoint, a web PowerPoint presentation for a client of mine who's been talking to hospital executives about the coming coding crisis caused by Obamacare. So it's okay if you don't know this because you don't run a hospital, but hospital executives and medical records department directors and so forth should know that postponed by one year by executive order, so, and I'm now not going to get the exact number right. It's in there, but I'll be close. So in October of next year, instead of October this year, but the codes that they use to build Medicare and insurance companies and so forth, they go from roughly 40,000 codes to 246,000 codes. So this is like a mess, okay? And like if you wait to deal with it until October of next year, you are going to be El Scruido, right? So when I started to work on this, I fortunately have a person who has a medical background. So I said, make some phone calls and just try and get a sense for me of how common knowledge this is with people running medical records departments and with HRD person in a hospital. And so the person sat on the phone for a day and came back and said, I wouldn't assume a lot of knowledge about this if I were you. There's sort of a vague awareness that this is going to change, but there's no real sense of the numbers. And so out of 22 completed conversations, four could quote some number of the size of the coming crisis. So I've got a crisis to work with. It's a great crowbar, but I can't assume that there's any knowledge about the crisis. The specter of loss, if I misspelled, no I didn't. The specter of loss, that'll keep somebody to stay tuned if you can scare the shit out of them, you know. Stay tuned because they're coming to get your guns. And I got new proof that they're coming to get your guns tomorrow. If you put that in front of a bunch of gun owners, you gained another three or four minutes to hold their attention. They're not clicking out. Benefits summary statement. So if you're going to lean on the benefit, then you put the benefit in there. So if you look old or new, and so some of the examples I'm going to show you as we go along, I've got my 1999 Peter Loewe script in there, and then I've got some current video sale of the other scripts. And you'll see I'm living with the same structure, haven't changed from 1999, you know. So the summary benefit tied to the who you are was if you are a salesman, I'm going to show you how to never have to cold prospect again. So that's a summary benefit of everything we do with magnetic marketing and salespeople. The ultimate benefit is they don't have to do any prospecting. Prospects come to them. If you're a business owner, it's a different summary benefit. Bribes, so you can bribe them to stay tuned. Tele-seminars, webcasts, the opening tease can include that during the course of the presentation, everybody's going to get a free e-book. Everybody's going to get a free download of a 17-point checklist that will immediately double, that will immediately cut their blood sugar level in half, that will immediately give them hard firm erections without having to take a pill. You want to stay tuned because we're going to let you have this for free. You can bribe them with everybody who's on the call, sometime in the call. You all have a code number that you got when you registered for the call, and we're going to have a drawing, and we're going to call out the code number, and somebody's going to win a car. So you can bribe them to stay tuned if you wish. And finally, you want to give them the direct specific instruction. I rarely do a stay tuned statement without saying stay tuned one way or another. You want to make sure you have a pad, you want to make sure you have a pen, because you're going to want to take notes. You want to stay tuned, we will often lie to them about the time, because in the next 22 minutes, and it's really 48, so you give them the specific instruction, but you want to establish why they need to stay tuned. Now, the more of what needs established that is established for you, the better. So here's some mistake speakers make who go speak at other people's events. They leave all the pre-event stuff up to the host. So you're going to go speak at Keller Williams, right? And so you deliver to them, here's my promotional shit. Here's my biography, here's some articles, here's the website, and then you're out until you show up. Well, you're not going to get as much of this as you could. First of all, they're going to democratize it. So if they have 18 speakers on a program, all 18 are getting the same brochure, real estate, all 18 are getting the same website, real estate. If they're going to send out 18 emails, one about each speaker, they're all going to be exactly the same length. If they have links, they're all going to have links. If they don't have links, none of them are going to have links. So when you seed the control to what gets established about you before you get an opportunity to make a presentation to people, you don't get there as much established as if you exercise a great deal of control. Because the only one who really cares, really cares about this being done well is you, not them. And even in environments where they're sharing in revenue you create from the presentation, be it doing it live, or they're all driving as affiliates, the association is driving people to your webcast, again, it doesn't make much difference. Even when they're sharing in the revenue, A, they care less about it than you do. In some cases they don't believe there's as much revenue there to be had as you know there is if we do everything right. And they may delegate it to people who still don't care at all. Revenue share doesn't affect Barbara, to whom all this work got handed off to. One of the things you will always see if you are speaking for someone else at their events, or you are having them drive to a tele-seminar, a webinar, whatever, and you give them generic information about you, they are going to put out the generic information. Nobody is going to sit and tweak it to be relevant to their audience. So if you look at our stuff, so currently what should be in your pause is the Info Summit magazine brochure. So everybody sends generic stuff. Everybody, I have yet, in all the years I've been on the other side of that, we're running an event, we hire a bunch of speakers, and speakers are coming, including the speakers who are coming to sell. So they should care deeply about what gets established for them before they get there. They send their generic stuff. Ours is not a generic audience. Now this time, if you look at the Info Summit piece, you will see I was able to do less of it for Goober than I would normally do for the celebrity, because it turns out he had a team of sales prevention attorneys who limited what we could write about him severely. And seriously enough that I would have ignored it, but we now have a team of sales prevention attorneys in Chicago as well. And there would be a risk of the guy stiffening and not showing in a problem. So if you look, and you compare it, say, to what I did about Gene Simmons or what I did about pretty much any other celebrity, you'll see I didn't do as good a job. It's because I couldn't do as good a job. But you'll see I still did a job. And if you look at everybody else and you study the copy, you'll see that I took this crowdfunding person who sent four paragraphs of generic copy about themselves, and I wrote it so to info marketers in our world there was relevance. No speaker does it from their side. I do it when I go speak. If I'm going to go work for Greg Stanley and go do a dental group, you were at the, Donna was at the, Dr. Galani was at the Greg Stanley thing in January. I don't give them my generic stuff. I go get doctor testimonials and I go get my health care references and I give them promotional material about me that is directly relevant to their audience. If I gave them all of it, so here's all the health care stuff and here's all the legal stuff and here's all the real estate stuff. I gave it to them on a big box with the generic stuff. They still wouldn't get it. They wouldn't do it. So the more of this that is established for you and in advance of the sales event for you, the better, but you don't want to rely on anybody else to do it well. And if you on the other side of the equation, and so you are hosting a webinar for Susan on how to use persuasive voice for real estate agents and you plan on making money on that, you can't settle for Susan gives you generic stuff about Susan and you pump it out to the real estate agents. You either got to make her go fix it or you got to say, okay, I got to take this generic stuff and I got to fix it to communicate to my people because I got to establish as much of this as possible for Susan before the time that she begins delivering a presentation to my real estate agents. And I'm telling you, you will never see a speaker do it. I've yet to see a speaker do it. So, you know, what is Mark send me? He sends me his two pocket folder with the same stuff in it that he sends to everybody and the same CD with eight pictures on it that he sends to that's what he sends. And nine out of ten places he would then go work would have shown what he sent to the audience before they arrived and he would not have as much established for him as if it was tweaked, which gets us to adapting to the nature of an audience. So, when you look at all this stuff that has to be in an open and all this stuff that has to be established, you do have to consider the nature of the audience. And if it doesn't change what is said and what gets established, it may at least change the order in which it is said and gets established because different order may matter to different people. So, I'll tell you the, so first of all, customized presentations are of course best for customized audiences. So, is Kim Walsh Phillips still here? There you are. So, this is, you will know who I'm speaking about. So, these are, these are, this is the president of and a vice president of a fairly large sales training company who go speak to a bunch of mailbox store owners and you know where you go pack and ship stuff. And they are selling a $90 a month coaching program on sales training. And their presentation to these mailbox shop owners is over 12 months, we're going to turn you into master salespeople. So, you'll be able to leave your shop and go out and run around and see all the businesses in your area and walk in and sell them on using your services. This is the rough equivalent of telling a room full of nuns that they will be much more effective in the world place if they just become hookers. This is roughly the same appeal. So, they're in front of a room full of people. The reason these folks own a pack and ship store is because it's a store and it has a counter. That's, that's its chief appeal is they get to come to the store and they get to stand behind a counter and people come in and give them money. That's, that's the business. That's what they want. They, and they really want to sing people all the time because they really want to be Ted Danson and cheers. That's what they want except they don't serve booze. So, they want to be at the bar and Norm comes in every day and sits there at the end of the counter with them and has lunch with them. And Barbara comes in on every Thursday with her Xerox copies to be made and they chat about Barbara's kid and dog and whatever. That's, that's what they want. You know this is the deal because every one of these franchisors gives them an apron to wear and hardly anybody goes out and makes sales calls wearing an apron. So, the guy comes in and he puts on his giant post that apron and he pulls his stool up and he sits down and he waits for people to come in. So, telling him you're going to take off the apron and you're going to become a master salesman and you're going to put on his tie and you're going to go out and you're going to walk in and sell bad message to market match. But you see it done all the time by the way. And so, not surprisingly. Now there's a way for them to have killed. The kill is, here's what we're going to do. For the first time ever and you guys have probably tried it before and not done very well, you're going to be able to have a commission compensated outside sales person who runs around and sells for you to all of the other businesses in your community because we are going to make him effective for 90 bucks a month. That everybody runs through the back of the room. What they did, the only people who went to the back of the room went there in a trickle during the presentation, leaving out the back of the room. Now, keep in mind, these are, this is the CEO of the largest sales training company in America who doesn't understand the fundamental message to market match. You know, I mean, a chiropractor can't effectively go out and knock on doors and make cold calls, but it's a damdably hard to sell idea to chiropractors. So you really got to give thought when you build your whole presentation, including your offer, as well as you're open to who is that audience and where are they in terms of relationship to you. So there's a difference, first of all, between a cold audience and a warm audience. Cold audience is obviously tougher, less has been established with a cold audience, so you do have to work a little more at getting it right. There's a difference between people being strangers to an idea and already having familiarity with that idea, having some level of comfort with that idea. So in the get rich world, the longest running get rich opportunity pitch in the history of the United States is get rich in real estate. It dates back to the Civil War. There were people putting people in rooms and selling them how to get rich in real estate during the Civil War. There was a southern tour and there was a northern tour, but there were people out there, see, there's the problem. Half the room has no historical perspective, and so see, that only works if you know that the Civil War was not between Croatia and France, that it was between the north and the south. And pretty much today, only old people from the south have that frame of reference. They're still flying the flag and they're, you know, so this is the oldest uninterrupted, effective get rich pitch ever. Why? Because everybody already has familiarity. They live in a house. They hope to live in a house. They know people who live in houses. And increasingly, so once you were kind of a little further along than the Civil War, almost everybody knows somebody, the dumb uncle, who owns three or four rental properties. And despite his bitching and moaning about the tenants, they know the uncle is making money. The next longest enduring, but much more difficult, always to make work, has been get rich in mail order. Because everybody has familiarity. And during the five decades that worked best, everybody got a lot of catalogs in the mail. Everybody did buy from mail order. Sears, Wishbook was in everybody's home. So you could do mail order pitches and there was already a sense of, I get it. It's why in get rich in internet, specific get rich in internet works better than general get rich in internet. So get rich on eBay works better than get rich on the internet. Because everybody has familiarity with eBay, that it works. And they know some dumb uncle who is so in crap on eBay and somehow making money, even though he's an idiot. So if they have familiarity, you can deal with this differently than if they don't have familiarity. Huge. How you open, how critical your open is, what you do with it, the quality of the source matters a lot. So if we drive dentists to a webcast about getting rich in real estate, has the perfect second opportunity for dentists. And we drive the great unwashed masses to a webcast about how to get rich in real estate for the great unwashed masses. Which person do you think we have a better chance with of keeping them paying attention for the first ten minutes? The dentist. By far. First of all, the pitch says it's for you. The other pitch has to say it's for everybody, which diminishes the appeal of the pitch. Secondly, the dentist has already learned how to pay attention. He had to go to college. He had to sit in a class. He had to pass a test somehow in order to be allowed to crawl into your mouth. He has to go to certification. He has to do that like every year to keep getting his certification. He actually has to pay attention when he does his work, for the most part. I mean, he's got to kind of, you know, have some ability to focus. He probably reads more. So Charlie Brown, who's got his GED, you know, and works at the convenience store, he is not as highly skilled in paying attention to anything. He's like out in the first six minutes of porn. I mean, he just can't stay focused, you know. So source matters a lot. A lot. Who we are driving to our presentations affects how we handle the open. Age or other demographics. Generally speaking, you've got to be much more concerned about this open with 25-year-olds than you do with 55-year-olds. They're just less patient. And 70-year-olds will pay attention for actually, you don't have to do much to keep in tune, because they don't move around much. Once they're, you're in front of them somehow, they pretty much, they still actually, so the stats, by the way, so I counted the other day, just to be sure. So I have 137 television channels. That's what I got. So Counting Premium and Russian and Japanese and every other day, I got 137. I probably got about 120 that you might watch, because I won't watch the Japanese station, generally speaking. So every once in a while, there's a home shopping show on the Japanese channel. It's just fascinating. You can't tell what anybody's saying, but it's really interesting to watch for a little bit, and watch them copy what we do on home shopping. It's really sort of interesting. But so I have all these channels. I watch like six television channels. I have serious radio in my car. I think there's 200 radio stations. I got four. I would be perfectly happy to just have the four. So they just did a big study. So the average homeowner has over 130 television channels. They watch 17. 70-year-olds watch four, and they still set it for the night, like they used to in the 50s. In the 50s, whatever evening news you watched, all the way into the 60s, that's pretty much where the TV channel stayed for the entire evening. So if you watched Walter Cronkite, you watched CBS until you went to bed, or switched over to Carson. That's sort of what went on. Well, they still behave that way. So if they tune in, they stay tuned in. 20-year-olds, mm-mm-mm. So you got to think about age as you do your open, and you got to think about the demographics, income level. All of that affects their ability and their willingness to stay tuned. You got to think about the difference between an audience accustomed to being sold by the media you are selling with versus an audience for whom it is foreign. So if you look at direct response advertising that sells to 55 and up, so the in-home walk-in bathtub, the mattress that does this, the doohickey that gets you up and down the steps. If you look at that advertising, none of it drives to webcast. It all drives to raise your hand, we'll send you a DVD. Don't think they haven't experimented. These are big companies actually run by smart people. Don't think they wouldn't love to eliminate the cost of the DVD. However, their customers are far more accustomed to watching television than they are to watching computer, and they buy and own DVDs. So they are much more accustomed to taking a DVD and putting it in the TV than they are anything else. So you have to think about what they're accustomed to in terms of media. There is some difference between inside physical walls, and if inside walls the time of day and what preceded it, and all of that. So that's our open. Then we also have to worry about open and into presentation. We do usually want a big idea that has the ability to stick. It's useful for there to be sort of one overriding big thing that everybody can get, either visually or said or stated, that is the umbrella for everything that is going to happen. Here is sort of your checklist. You want it simple, you want it telegraphic. So a successful infomation I worked on years ago, which I've now recycled. You'll see me use it in an example. I'll show you was Gary Cochran show, get a second paycheck without a second job. You got it. It's a big idea, and the person immediately gets it. I go to work, I get a $200 a week check for work. If I could get a second one without getting a second job, that would be really cool. They get it. That's a big idea that sticks. It's very simple, it's very direct, it's not particularly informative, but it's very big and it's very quick. So there's simplicity by exclusion. So simplicity by exclusion is leaving out a whole lot of detail and just getting to the big idea and who it's for. There's simplicity by statement. So again, one that we had on for eight years was turned $399 into $3,990 every weekend. The person gets it. Weekend, they know what a weekend is, and they know what $399 is, and if you told them ten times $399 every weekend, that they wouldn't get, because that requires them to do math. And most people are no good at math, so you have to say it for them, but that's simplicity by statement. Big whack on the side of the head. So there's a campaign running now. You'll see it. Even poor people in Okinawa outlive rich people in America because of their diet. Now, most people don't know where Okinawa is, but they generally know it's not where they are. It's an exotic, foreign-sounding thing, right? And they probably instantly think of it as kind of a poor place because any place that's not America to an American is a poor place, and these people are outliving us because of their diet. Not because of doctors and hospitals and pills, because of their diet. So that's kind of a big whack, powerful image or word picture. So everybody used to laugh about it, but there was an infomercial that ran. Saturday Night Live did a parody of it. The guy eventually went to jail, but not because of the images, but there was a very successful infomercial that ran for years about Get Rich in Real Estate. The guy's name was Tommy Vu, and the show literally opened with Tommy in a hot tub with four really hot babes and bikinis around him. And people laughed their ass off. The show in its time brought in about $100 million. It lasted for five years or so, and everybody talked about it. I mean, it was either everybody saw it or they were telling their buddies, you got to watch for this infomercial because there's this little Chinese guy who we all know couldn't possibly get laid, and he's in this hot tub with these four babes, and it's all because he's Get Rich. So this was like a national conversation. It was a very powerful image, and so you can get a lot of open power if you figure out how to do a big image as part of your open. Specificity, okay? So specific promises, numbers, number of days, that can work as a sticky idea, and triggering one of the key emotions works as a... So as you know, the most successful newsletter promotion may be of all time, but certainly of recent years has been an advertising campaign that drives to an online video for End of America. If you saw radio ads, TV ads, direct mail, this thing's huge, and in a slightly tweaked incarnation, it is still running. If you want to see the original, you can go to YouTube and find it. But so endofamerica.com, that's a big sticky idea, right? And it triggers alarm, right? Carnegie Dales' old How to Win Friends and Influence People was a big sticky idea, and it was about anti-embarrassment and a desire for popularity. So as you go to work on openings, by the way, I have a couple of resources for you. The first one is something you can use day in, day out as you create opens. It's Masterson's book on great leads. How many of you have this book? Good for you, okay, but a bunch of you don't. So go to Amazon, get this book. This will help you a lot with opens. Then if you don't have How to Write a Great Adversity, it's the best headline sheet. There's 150 headline templates in it, which if you're going to write three opening statements, you're writing three headlines. Sort of more conceptual is the Made to Stick book and the Fascinate book by Sally. So here's a bunch of different ways to do ideas that stick. One is by the numbers, okay? Number is help people hold on to an idea. That's why you see them used so much. So you have 17 laws of success. You have seven habits of highly successful people. A health talk is always difficult to do in the rally environment. We had for all but two of the years that I was on the success tour. We had a health speaker in the afternoon. His name was Ted Brower. I'm sure you can Google Ted and probably find presentations. So Ted did the 10 foods never to eat. And it's a sticky idea. There's 10 and I should never eat them. And he was really effective and others who tried in his subject matter with us were not particularly effective. That's a great presentation. Ted for years made a seven-figure income never leaving his house just doing radio interviews. So he was very adept at 10 foods never to eat, doing a radio interview, driving people first to a phone room and then to a website as time progressed and selling $400, $500, $600 boxes of information. So foods never to eat is nowhere near as impactful as sticking a number on it. 10 foods never to eat. Habits of highly successful people is nowhere as impactful as seven habits of highly successful people. So you almost never see great advertising without a number in it. Another way to make ideas stick is by alphabet and acronyms. And we all cringe at this. And you know, but it works and it helps people grasp an idea. Objects. So you see a lot of presenters over the years use an object to hold attention and make an idea stick. If you saw Zig speak, you almost never saw him speak without the big giant pump on stage. That thing had to be shipped from place to place to place to place. It actually was metal. It was heavy. I don't know what it cost in a year to ship that damn thing from place to place to place for the low events. He had to have two of them because we did Tuesdays and Thursdays and you couldn't count on getting the thing from noon Tuesday to the morning of Thursday. So there were two moving around the country all the time. Zig was no dummy. He'd already figured out long ago he saw more stuff with the pump than without the pump. So you can do a lot. You'll always see the weight loss people. The smart ones doing the big lump of rubber fat. This is how much. That was done 30 years ago. I saw Dr. Oz do it a few months ago. So somewhere there's a company that makes big giant rubber globs of fat that has the guy who's probably gotten rich over the years. His kids are all clipping annuity coupons because they've sold millions of these bottles. But you'll see it done because you know people get it. So objects can help you a lot. Physical objects on stage on a set on a webcast set. One of my kind of gripes about webinars and webcasts is people don't build sets. But it's TV so we should build sets. Ideas that stick by symbolism. You may remember during the first campaign there was some talk about a commercial in which Obama was speaking to you from a house inside a little normal middle of the road mainstream American house. And there's a window behind him. And the window. He didn't have a cross in it. It's just that the things that hold the window together form the cross. It was right over his shoulder and it was lit. So it was a little brighter than everything else that was behind his shoulder. Now most people didn't notice it consciously. The media picked up on it and then everybody talked about it. But there's only three million people that pay attention to the media. But it was almost certainly done deliberately. At the time as you know he was heavily dealing with is this guy a Muslim. So it was whoever did it deliberately did it for good reason. So symbolism matters a lot. For many years. I guess probably until maybe the last 20. Sort of the symbol of having made it in America was driving a Cadillac. So almost all get rich marketing of any kind used Cadillacs. And direct selling put people in Cadillacs. Originally Mary Kay was all you didn't get a choice. You got a pink Cadillac. You didn't get a Lincoln. You didn't get a Buick. You didn't get a Prius. You got a damn Cadillac. Because that was symbolic of making it in America is finally having a Cadillac in the driveway. Now that has fallen apart and gone away. It doesn't matter anymore much one way or the other. But there are these symbols that matter to people. And in a second I'm going to show you a clip of a information we did for a company called Travel Masters. And I'm just going to show you a little clip because there's symbolism of happy family vacation occurring sort of behind what else is going on in the show. Ideas that stick by graphic and dramatic images. So everybody knows the car crash commercials with the dummy and the thing crashes into the wall. Right. Everybody knows that if you have a certain age you know one of the most one of the first successful over the counter drug commercials was Anison which had a hammer inside a person's head pounding away. It's a great graphic image right in direct sales which I'm going to show you the end of my all time favorite and it is old. They only stopped using this however only about eight or nine years ago in the fire alarm business. It's famous. It's a fire alarm video. So when the fire alarm salesman came and still in some market areas comes to your house to sell you fire alarms. They show you this film and they sit there with you at the kitchen table so you watch the film driving people to it on a webcast. By the way it doesn't work at all because they stop watching it slow as you'll see. And I'm only going to show you the punchline part. It's 30 minutes long. I'm not showing you the 30 minutes and it's a very slow paced build to one whack at the end. It's called in the industry the crying dog film. So spoiler alert. It is all about at the end the crying dog and it's ponderous but it is a very powerful image roughly a third of the people who see it in the sales environment. The tear up the spouse looks at the other one and they are tears are coming out of their eyes to and it clinches the fire alarm sale. The fire alarm business by the way is about three to eight thousand dollar sales and their heat detectors. So the three minute explanation about that you all have smoke detectors. Here we are not protected by smoke detectors. You are up in your sleeping rooms because by law they have to have them but if fire breaks out look up there at the sprinklers. If fire breaks out in here sprinklers are going to go on. They're detected by heat. They're not detected by smoke. If they wait to be detected by smoke by the way we all burn to death. So contrary to what the smoke detector guys tell you two out of three fires reach maximum heat before they generate much smoke. So these are heat detectors up there. What happens is when they detect a certain amount of heat they pop off which triggers the sprinkler system. So a home heat detector is a metal can. You could go home and build one. It's a round metal can about the size of a smoke detector and it mounts on the wall. And what's inside it is a big coiled metal spring. And what's on the outside says mechanical device no batteries no electric no nothing. What's on the outside of it is what are those. And when a certain amount of heat happens it pops off which lets the coiled spring loose which unwinds in the can making a hell of a racket. That's what a heat detector is. And they're sold for three to eight thousand bucks to do an average size house. That's what this business is. And it's very profitable business by the way because it's a metal can with a coiled spring inside it. And at one time by the way this was a huge industry in America. Direct selling is less you know and the smoke detectors became a problem and all of that. But it still exists by the way. There's two companies. They're fairly substantial. They probably have each ten thousand dealers out in the marketplace. They each have four or five sales people. So it's probably fifty thousand people in this business today still going to somebody's house at night. And in some cases showing the more modern version of the crying dog film. But the new one is nowhere near as good as this one. So dramatic images are very very powerful. And we tend to neglect them in producing video today doing webcast today. Even doing live audience presentations in much the same way. That in print we tend not to pay as much attention to the photographs as we should. Print is driven by copywriters. And so we really think about the language more than we think about the photographic images. Video tends to be driven by the script writers in direct response. So we think about the scripts not as much about the images. But these images are very very powerful. So you're ready to go. Okay. This is travel. Here comes Chris. We'll finish this later. Okay. Hi. Welcome to the travel opportunity show where four things are going to happen. Number one you're going to discover how all the barriers and obstacles to having a part-time business of your own have been removed. So that it's easy and profitable for you to become part of the booming vacation travel business. Two you'll meet this folks person for the large well-known travel company ready to set you up in business. And you'll meet successful people already enjoying the benefits of this opportunity. Three we're going to go visit great vacation spots. And four you'll be given a totally free offer. I'm sure you'll want to take advantage of. I'll be right back to start this show. So don't go away. And I have made close to two thousand five hundred dollars which is good because I earned the actually having fun. Through the networking program in the last 60 days I've made almost twelve thousand dollars. I'm a teacher by profession and part-time through the networking program. I've earned twelve thousand in three months. Travel the most glamorous exciting business on earth from resorts to campgrounds to condos in Florida. America loves to travel. Now you can participate in this multi-billion dollar industry without any investment or risk. Without becoming a travel agent with a spare time home-based business you can earn an excellent income. The only business we know that you can make money while having fun. In fact we call it the business of having fun. The character of a house and home is more apparent at night. The family is together. Isolated and safe from the dark outside. Everyone is relaxed. Past day is just a memory. Now we are safe at home. The place where nothing bad ever happened. Okay, Tiger, it's time for you and your sister to go to bed now. Come on. What's that? A water crystal is better some place. It's alright, I'll find it later. Go on. Sit down with your children. You too. Go on. They plan together. Plan two escape routes from every route. Teach children to escape at once. Don't let them try to put out the fire or stop to gather up possessions. Practice family fire drills regularly. Knowing what to do. Water crystal. Yesterday he lost it on the roof. He has he's naked? Yes, we had. We'll get burned up soon. Bye Bynuch reciprocity. He loses secrets. All he needs is political security in general. I think I missed my turn. Come on, do it. That's a good song. That's a good song. Hey, outside. You go outside. You don't go in there. Come on, come on. Come on. Come on. Outside. Outside you go. Come on. Come on. Come on. Fire in a sofa may smolder for hours before it breaks into open flame. You can fall asleep in a chair or on a sofa and never wake up. Lethal gasses are released by the smoldering fire. The gasses themselves can suffocate you. Once free on the loose, fire is incredibly swift. It can run, climb, leap, it can even fly. Large buildings are often gutted in 10 minutes. A house can go in five or even three. In a burning room, the heated lethal gasses usually rise to the ceiling. Moments later, the whole upper area of the room is filled with them. By the time you awaken, the killing gasses may be just above you. Well, Colonel, it's plain as day what happened. The fire started over there in the sofa. Probably a cigarette that's smoldered for hours and they never woke up in time. We found the father right here, Colonel, by the bedroom window. The indications are that he rose straight up out of bed and dropped. He might have made it if he'd had cropped low, kept his head down. The mother was right over there on the floor. Now, evidently, she was going after the kids and... How about the kids? Well, let's go into the bedroom, Colonel, and I'll show you. Now, the little boy slept in the top bunk and he died in his sleep. The little girl, she went for the front door. She almost made it too because they found her in the living room. Couldn't she get out the window? Well, that's what she should have done. The kids don't stop to think. They do the things they're used to, and all she knew is that people go out through doors. Where a terrible price to pay, certainly is.
